subtitle: Between Two Lions: Bindusara and the Quiet Empire

panels.shot: low-angle medium-wide of a solemn king on a palace terrace, empire stretching into hazy distance
panels.action: Bindusara Amitraghata stands in regal stillness on the high stone terrace of Pataliputra, hands resting on the hilt of a sheathed sword, the wide Ganga plain and distant caravan roads fading into morning mist behind him.

dialogue.text: Between Chandragupta and Ashoka stands Bindusara Amitraghata, the quiet Maurya who held a vast empire together while the world of India met the world of the Greeks.

i18n.hi: चंद्रगुप्त और अशोक के बीच खड़े हैं बिंदुसार अमित्रघात, वह शांत मौर्य जिसने ग्रीक और भारत की दुनिया को मिलते हुए एक विशाल साम्राज्य जोड़े रखा।

panels.shot: wide establishing shot of Pataliputra palace complex from above
panels.action: From a bird’s‑eye view, the long timbered halls and pillared audience courts of Pataliputra stretch along the river, guards and scribes like tiny ants as Bindusara’s figure is seen seated on the central throne platform beneath a high wooden canopy.

dialogue.text: Around 297 BCE, Chandragupta Maurya steps back from the throne. In the timbered halls of Pataliputra, his son Bindusara inherits not a conquest to win, but an empire to keep from breaking.

i18n.hi: लगभग 297 ईसा‑पूर्व, चंद्रगुप्त मौर्य सिंहासन छोड़ते हैं। पाटलिपुत्र के लकड़ीदार महलों में, उनका बेटा बिंदुसार जीतने नहीं, बचाकर रखने वाला साम्राज्य पाता है।

panels.shot: medium close-up profile of Bindusara seated on the lion throne, half in shadow
panels.action: Bindusara sits on a carved lion‑legged throne, his face lit on one side by lamp‑light and shadowed on the other, one hand resting on a rolled palm‑leaf report, the other loosely gripping the armrest.

dialogue.text: The records are almost silent. No rock declares his victories, no pillar proclaims his words. History remembers his name through others’ voices: Puranic lists, Buddhist tales, and a Greek banquet book.

i18n.hi: दस्तावेज़ लगभग खामोश हैं। कोई शिला उसकी जीत नहीं गाती, कोई स्तंभ उसके वचन नहीं दोहराता। उसका नाम दूसरों की जुबान से बचा है—पुराण, बौद्ध कथाएँ और एक यूनानी भोज‑पुस्तक।

panels.shot: tight close-up on Bindusara’s eyes as he studies a palm-leaf map
panels.action: A close view shows Bindusara’s steady eyes lowered to a palm‑leaf map etched with faint lines for provinces and roads, small stone counters marking governors and garrisons scattered from the northwest to the eastern sea.

dialogue.text: He is Amitraghata to the Greeks, Amitrochates, ‘slayer of enemies’. Yet the enemies he faces most often are distance, delay, restless princes and proud provincial elites.

i18n.hi: यूनानियों के लिए वह अमित्रघात, अमित्रोखातेस, ‘शत्रु‑संहारक’। पर उसके असली दुश्मन हैं दूरी, देरी, बेचैन कुमार और अकड़े हुए प्रांतीय सरदार।

panels.shot: interior wide shot of royal council hall with Bindusara on the throne and ministers at a respectful distance
panels.action: In the council hall, Bindusara sits upright as ministers and scribes stand in two careful rows, some in fine cotton, others in rougher cloth, each holding tablets or folded palm leaves, a faint tension in their shoulders as they wait for his word.

dialogue.text: The empire stretches from the northwest frontier to the Bay of Bengal. Satraps inherited from Seleucus, republican clans in the hills, old mahajanapada elites in cities—all must feel the king’s presence without constant war.

i18n.hi: साम्राज्य उत्तर‑पश्चिम सीमा से बंगाल की खाड़ी तक फैला है। सेल्युकस के छोड़े हुए सत्राप, पहाड़ी गणराज्य और नगरों के पुराने महाजनपद—सबको बिना लगातार युद्ध के राजा की मौजूदगी महसूस होनी है।

panels.shot: medium shot of Bindusara listening as a messenger bows, dusty from travel
panels.action: A messenger, clothes dusty from the road, bows low before the throne, while Bindusara leans slightly forward, brows furrowed, listening with calm intensity, the faintest crease at the corner of his mouth.

dialogue.text: Our sources do not list his campaigns. Later storytellers boast that he conquered ‘the land between two seas’, but the earth and stone remain silent. What we can see is his steady hand on inherited provinces.

i18n.hi: हमारे स्रोत उसकी लड़ाइयाँ नहीं गिनाते। बाद के किस्सागो कहते हैं उसने ‘दो समुद्रों के बीच’ सब जीता, पर धरती‑पत्थर चुप हैं। जो दिखता है, वह है उसकी पकड़ में रहे पुराने प्रांत।

panels.shot: medium interior shot in a more relaxed private chamber, Bindusara with a foreign envoy and gifts on a low table
panels.action: In a quieter chamber hung with simple cotton drapes, Bindusara sits on a low seat opposite a bearded western envoy in a long cloak, between them a table with strange bronze cups, a sealed clay amphora, and a small basket of unfamiliar dried fruit.

dialogue.text: Beyond the northwest frontier sits the Seleucid realm. Chandragupta once traded war for a marriage alliance and elephants. Now, under Antiochus I, the tie must be kept alive with gifts, courtesies, and questions.

i18n.hi: उत्तर‑पश्चिम सीमा के पार है सेल्युसिड साम्राज्य। कभी चंद्रगुप्त ने युद्ध छोड़ विवाह‑संधि और हाथियों का सौदा किया था। अब अंतियोकस प्रथम के समय, इस डोर को भेंटों, अदब और सवालों से जिंदा रखना है।

panels.shot: over-the-shoulder shot from behind Bindusara as he dictates to a scribe, looking out a lattice toward the western horizon
panels.action: Seen from behind Bindusara’s shoulder, a scribe on the floor scratches on a palm leaf as the king dictates, his gaze turned through a lattice window toward the faint orange light of a western sunset beyond the Ganga.

dialogue.text: Centuries later, Athenaeus will tell a story: that Bindusara wrote to Antiochus I, asking for sweet wine, dried figs, and a Greek sophist. The tale may be polished in retelling, but it hints at real contact.

i18n.hi: सदियों बाद, अथेनायुस एक कथा सुनाएगा: कि बिंदुसार ने अंतियोकस प्रथम को लिखा, मीठी मदिरा, सूखी अंजीर और एक यूनानी सोफिस्ट माँगा। कहानी घिस‑पिट कर चमकाई गई हो, पर असली संपर्क की झलक देती है।

panels.shot: medium shot, Bindusara dictating with a faint ironic smile while the scribe looks up in surprise
panels.action: Bindusara leans back slightly, a rare, small smile at one corner of his mouth, as the scribe pauses mid‑stroke and looks up, surprised by the unusual list he is being asked to record.

dialogue.speaker: bindusara
dialogue.text: Write that the king of the Yavanas should send sweet wine and dried figs.

i18n.hi: लिखो कि यवनों का राजा मीठी मदिरा और सूखी अंजीर भेजे।

panels.shot: closer shot focusing on the scribe’s hand inscribing the request, Bindusara’s shadow falling over the leaf
panels.action: The frame tightens on the scribe’s hand as he etches careful characters for wine and figs into the palm leaf, Bindusara’s shadow falling long and steady over the writing.

dialogue.text: To Indian ears, these are luxuries from afar, signs of refinement and curiosity. To Greek readers, they paint an eastern king who knows the pleasures of their world and can afford to ask.

i18n.hi: भारतीय कानों को ये दूर देश की नायाब चीजें हैं—रस और जिज्ञासा की निशानी। यूनानी पाठकों को दिखता है एक पूरब का राजा, जो उनकी दुनिया के सुख जानता है और बेझिझक माँग सकता है।

panels.shot: medium shot showing Bindusara now serious again as he adds the request for a philosopher
panels.action: Bindusara’s face turns grave again as he raises a hand, the scribe’s stylus hovering above the leaf, waiting for the final and most unusual part of the message.

dialogue.speaker: bindusara
dialogue.text: And add this: send me also a man trained in your wisdom, a philosopher.

i18n.hi: और यह भी जोड़ो: मुझे तुम्हारी विद्या में दीक्षित एक पुरुष भेजो, एक दार्शनिक।

panels.shot: transition wide shot of a caravan departing Pataliputra toward the northwest, empire roads stretching ahead
panels.action: A long line of pack animals and escorts moves out from Pataliputra’s northern gate, dust rising behind them as they follow the royal road toward the distant northwest, the river gleaming like a silver band beside them.

dialogue.text: Whether the words were exactly as Athenaeus records, the caravan roads carried messages and gifts. Indian textiles and spices went west; foreign wines and stories came east, weaving new threads into Mauryan life.

i18n.hi: शब्द भले ठीक वैसा रहे हों या नहीं, कारवाँ‑राहों पर संदेश और भेंटें चलती रहीं। भारतीय वस्त्र और मसाले पश्चिम गए, विदेशी मदिरा और किस्से पूरब लौटे, मौर्य जीवन में नई डोरें बुनते हुए।

panels.shot: interior wide shot of Antiochus I’s hall in a Seleucid city, with courtiers and Greek philosophers present
panels.action: In a stone‑pillared hall adorned with Greek motifs, Antiochus I sits among courtiers, a scroll opened in his hand, while, nearby, bearded philosophers in simple cloaks stand apart, one looking on with wary interest.

dialogue.text: In a faraway court—perhaps Sardis, perhaps Antioch—Antiochus I hears of the eastern king’s request. Wine and figs can be shipped. A philosopher, Greek tradition insists, is no commodity.

i18n.hi: किसी दूर दरबार—शायद सार्डिस, शायद अंतियोक—में अंतियोकस प्रथम को पूरब के राजा की यह फरियाद सुनाई पड़ती है। मदिरा और अंजीर तो चल पड़ें, पर यूनानी परंपरा कहती है—दार्शनिक बिकाऊ नहीं।

panels.shot: medium shot focusing on a Greek courtier half-rising as if to volunteer, restrained by a hand on his arm
panels.action: A cloaked Greek scholar half‑rises from his place, eyes alight as if tempted by the call of a distant land, while a courtier’s hand rests firmly on his arm, holding him back.

dialogue.text: Athenaeus’ story has Antiochus refuse: philosophers are not for export. True or embellished, the image endures—a line drawn around Greek learning, even as Greek goods cross that line freely.

i18n.hi: अथेनायुस की कथा में अंतियोकस इंकार करता है: दार्शनिक निर्यात नहीं होते। सच हो या सजावटी, तस्वीर टिक जाती है—जहाँ यूनानी विद्या पर रेखा खिंची है, पर यूनानी माल बेधड़क उसे पार करता है।

panels.shot: interior medium shot of Bindusara receiving amphorae and baskets in a storeroom, examining a fig thoughtfully
panels.action: Back in Pataliputra, Bindusara stands in a cool storeroom as attendants open clay amphorae and baskets; he lifts a dried fig between his fingers, studying its wrinkled surface before tasting it, his expression unreadable.

dialogue.text: The wine and figs may indeed have arrived. The philosopher did not—if he was ever truly requested. Yet in that absence lies the echo of a question: what knowledge can be traded, and what must remain rooted?

i18n.hi: मदिरा और अंजीर शायद सचमुच पहुँची भी हों। दार्शनिक नहीं पहुँचा—अगर सच में माँगा भी गया हो। पर उसी कमी में एक प्रश्न की गूँज बची रहती है: कौन‑सी विद्या व्यापारी बन सकती है, और कौन जड़ से बँधी रहती है?

panels.shot: medium shot of Bindusara in discussion with Indian scholars and ascetics in an open courtyard
panels.action: In an open palace courtyard shaded by a fig tree, Bindusara sits on a low platform surrounded by Indian brahmanas and shramana ascetics, each distinct in dress and bearing, as they debate, hands gesturing, while the king listens in calm silence.

dialogue.text: Within his own realm, Bindusara’s court holds many voices—Vedic ritualists, wandering renunciants, merchants of ideas as well as goods. His son Ashoka will later favor the dhamma of the Buddha, but the seedbed is already dense.

i18n.hi: अपने ही दरबार में बिंदुसार के आस‑पास कई आवाजें हैं—वैदिक ऋत्विज, घूमते संन्यासी, और विचारों के सौदागर। उसका बेटा अशोक बाद में बुद्ध के धम्म की ओर झुकेगा, पर बीज‑भूमि तो पहले ही घनी है।

panels.shot: wide shot of a Deccan landscape with a Mauryan garrison atop a hill, Bindusara’s standard flying
panels.action: A fortified hilltop in the Deccan rises over scrub and fields, a modest Mauryan garrison within, the royal standard fluttering in the wind as village life continues below under the watchful presence of distant imperial power.

dialogue.text: Later Tibetan chroniclers will claim he conquered the entire south, between the ‘two seas’. Our evidence does not support it. What is visible is something quieter: Mauryan influence edging into the Deccan, more through presence than storm.

i18n.hi: बाद के तिब्बती इतिहास कहेंगे कि उसने ‘दो समुद्रों के बीच’ पूरा दक्षिण मसल डाला। सबूत कुछ और कहते हैं—धीमी मौजूदगी: मौर्य पहुँच दक्कन तक रेंगती हुई, तूफ़ान से ज़्यादा बस उपस्थिति से।

panels.shot: intimate close-up of Bindusara older, in a dim chamber, looking at a small figurine or token representing his heir
panels.action: An older Bindusara sits alone in a dim chamber, lines deepened on his face, turning a small carved figurine of a lion in his hands, his gaze distant as if weighing the future of his sons and the state they will inherit.

dialogue.text: The family traditions we have—Buddhist and Sri Lankan—speak of sons contending, of Ashoka sent to govern distant provinces. Behind their drama stands Bindusara, trying to bind princes and provinces into a single fabric.

i18n.hi: जो पारिवारिक परंपराएँ हमारे पास हैं—बौद्ध और श्रीलंकाई—वे बेटों की खींचतान, दूर प्रांतों में अशोक की नियुक्ति की बात करती हैं। उनके नाटकों के पीछे खड़े हैं बिंदुसार, जो कुमारों और प्रांतों को एक कपड़े में पिरोना चाहते हैं।

panels.shot: medium shot of Bindusara on his throne for the last time, ministers around him fading into darkness at the edges
panels.action: Bindusara sits on the lion throne one last time, shoulders slightly stooped yet gaze still firm, while ministers stand in a semicircle that seems to recede into shadow at the edges, as if the scene itself is slipping from memory.

dialogue.text: Around 272 BCE, his reign ends. No inscription marks his passing. Yet the frame he maintained—a functioning bureaucracy, a network of roads and outposts—will become the stage on which Ashoka’s better‑known drama unfolds.

i18n.hi: लगभग 272 ईसा‑पूर्व, उनका राज ख़त्म होता है। कोई शिलालेख उनकी विदाई नहीं लिखता। पर जो ढांचा उन्होंने साधा—चलता हुआ तंत्र, सड़कों और चौकियों का जाल—यही बाद में अशोक के मशहूर नाटक का मंच बनेगा।

panels.shot: closing wide shot blending Bindusara’s silhouette between faint figures of Chandragupta and Ashoka on either side, with trade routes and frontiers sketched behind
panels.action: Against a muted backdrop of flowing roads, frontier forts and distant ships, Bindusara’s central silhouette stands between the more distinct, flanking figures of Chandragupta and Ashoka, his outline quieter yet anchoring the composition.

dialogue.text: Bindusara’s legacy is not in grand battles, but in continuity: between a founder who seized power and a son who moralized it, he kept the Mauryan world intact while India and the Hellenistic West learned to look toward each other.

i18n.hi: बिंदुसार की विरासत किसी विराट युद्ध में नहीं, बल्कि सिलसिले में है: एक संस्थापक की ताकत और एक पुत्र की नैतिकता के बीच, उसने मौर्य दुनिया को जोड़े रखा, जबकि भारत और हेलेनिस्टिक पश्चिम एक‑दूसरे की ओर देखना सीख रहे थे।

title_i18n.hi: बिंदुसार की अंजीर, मदिरा और दार्शनिक

subtitle_i18n.en: Between Two Lions: Bindusara and the Quiet Empire
subtitle_i18n.hi: दो शेरों के बीच: बिंदुसार और खामोश साम्राज्य
